agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is being scoped to allow customers visibility into their delivery's progress. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, while Operations demands no location sharing at all, citing driver retention risk.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Last-Mile Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"The driver or delivery person physically transporting a package from origin to customer."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates as reported by their device, updated on a frequent interval during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to a customer's delivery address."},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The level of precision at which a courier's position is exposed — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to coarse zone-level proximity."},{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — a derived metric communicating when the delivery is expected, without necessarily exposing raw location."},{"term":"Driver retention","definition":"The operational metric measuring the ability to keep couriers on the platform; directly affected by working conditions and perceived surveillance."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier's GPS signal is lost or unreliable mid-delivery","expected_behavior":"Cannot be defined until the core contradiction is resolved — but any solution must degrade gracefully rather than showing stale or inaccurate positions."},{"scenario":"Customer uses location data to intercept or confront courier en route","expected_behavior":"This is the safety concern underlying Operations' objection. Any resolution must account for courier physical safety."},{"scenario":"Courier is making multiple stops on a route and customer sees them 'going the wrong way'","expected_behavior":"Exact GPS creates false perception of problems; abstract progress indicators may be more appropriate but this depends on resolution."},{"scenario":"Regulatory jurisdiction requires data minimization (e.g., GDPR)","expected_behavior":"Sharing exact GPS coordinates of an employee with a third party has privacy and compliance implications that neither team has surfaced."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If drivers leave due to surveillance concerns, recruitment and onboarding costs spike (industry avg $3K-$5K per driver). If customers churn due to lack of visibility, customer acquisition cost is wasted. Both paths have direct revenue impact — the resolution determines which cost is avoided.","operational":"Driver retention is an existential operational concern for a courier business. A 10% increase in driver attrition can cascade into missed SLAs, longer delivery times, and degraded service quality — which ironically destroys the customer experience that Customer Success is trying to protect.","user":"Customers want confidence their package is coming. Operations data from the industry consistently shows customers care more about accurate ETAs than raw GPS pins. The actual user need may be 'certainty of arrival' not 'surveillance capability.'","risk":"CRITICAL: Exposing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers creates legal liability (worker privacy, GDPR/CCPA data minimization principles, potential physical safety incidents). This risk has not been evaluated by either team.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Want delivery confidence and transparency. May be fully satisfied by accurate ETA + progress stages rather than exact GPS. Exact GPS may actually increase anxiety (watching a pin not move in traffic)."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected. Exact GPS sharing creates perceived and real surveillance. Industry data shows this is a top-3 driver dissatisfaction factor. Driver supply is the bottleneck in most courier businesses."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Their KPIs around NPS and satisfaction are at stake. But their proposed solution (exact GPS) may not be the only or best way to achieve their actual goal."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Owns driver retention and delivery capacity. Their blanket 'no location ever' stance may also be overcorrected — some form of progress visibility is industry standard."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED. Sharing worker GPS with third parties has privacy law implications in most jurisdictions. This stakeholder is missing from the conversation."},{"group":"Product/Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. The resolution is a strategic business decision, not a feature decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is critically low because two authoritative stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory requirements, both framed as non-negotiable. No executive arbitration has occurred. A key stakeholder (Legal/Compliance) has not been consulted. The spec as given is unimplementable — any implementation would violate one team's stated requirement. Additionally, there is likely a third path (abstracted progress/ETA) that neither team has proposed, which requires product strategy input.","high_confidence_areas":["This is a genuine business contradiction that cannot be resolved at the engineering level","Driver retention is a legitimate existential concern for courier businesses","Customer desire for delivery visibility is a real and valid need","Exact GPS coordinates are not the only way to provide delivery transparency","Legal/privacy review is required before any location-sharing feature ships"],"low_confidence_areas":["What the actual executive-level business priority is (customer acquisition vs driver retention)","Whether a compromise solution (e.g., ETA-based tracking, zone-level proximity) would satisfy Customer Success","The specific regulatory requirements in the jurisdictions this service operates in","Whether drivers would accept any form of location sharing, even abstracted"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith based on their domain expertise","There is no existing executive mandate that resolves this conflict","The business operates in jurisdictions where worker privacy laws apply","Driver supply is a constrained resource for this business"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS location sharing with customers during delivery. Operations requires that courier location must NEVER be shown to customers. These are mutually exclusive requirements from teams of equal authority. This cannot be resolved by engineering — it requires executive arbitration and likely a product strategy decision that balances customer transparency against driver retention and privacy.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers/Drivers","Customer Success team","Operations team","Legal/Compliance","Executive leadership"],"options":["A: Side with Customer Success — show exact GPS. Risk: accelerated driver attrition, potential legal liability, operational collapse.","B: Side with Operations — show nothing. Risk: competitive disadvantage, customer churn, Customer Success KPIs suffer.","C: Find a middle path — show abstracted progress (ETA, delivery stages like 'Courier is nearby', zone-level proximity) that gives customers confidence without surveilling drivers. This is the industry-standard approach used by UPS, FedEx, and most mature logistics platforms.","D: Make it driver-opt-in — couriers choose whether to share location, customers see ETA regardless. Respects driver autonomy while offering transparency."],"recommendation":"Option C, validated by Legal review. The industry has already solved this: customers get accurate ETAs and stage-based progress ('Order picked up', 'Courier en route', 'Courier nearby', 'Arriving in 2 minutes') which satisfies the transparency need without exposing exact GPS. This is not a compromise — it is genuinely better UX than a raw GPS pin. However, this recommendation MUST be arbitrated by executive leadership, not decided by engineering. Escalate immediately."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Legal and Compliance have not been consulted on sharing employee GPS coordinates with third-party customers. In GDPR, CCPA, and many labor law jurisdictions, this has significant legal implications including potential requirement for explicit worker consent, data minimization obligations, and liability for safety incidents enabled by location sharing.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers/Drivers","Executive leadership"],"options":["Proceed without legal review (high risk)","Block feature until legal review completes (recommended)"],"recommendation":"Block any implementation until Legal/Compliance provides written guidance on permissible location data sharing with customers."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Contradictory requirements from equally-authoritative stakeholders make any implementation incorrect by definition — building either version violates a stated business requirement.","business_rule_broken":"Cannot define business rules when the two stakeholder teams disagree on the fundamental rule: 'show location' vs 'never show location'."},{"violation":"Missing Legal/Compliance review for sharing worker location data with third parties.","business_rule_broken":"Data privacy and worker protection regulations have not been evaluated. Any implementation without this review carries unquantified legal risk."}]}
